title: Five-Year Clinical Outcomes With Nivolumab Plus Ipilimumab Versus Chemotherapy as First-Line Treatment for Unresectable Pleural Mesothelioma in CheckMate 743.
abstract: In this 5-year follow-up from the CheckMate 743 study in patients with unresectable pleural mesothelioma (PM), we evaluated updated efficacy and safety outcomes and biomarkers and performed treatment-switching analyses with first-line nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy. With a median follow-up of 66.8 months, nivolumab plus ipilimumab demonstrated continued overall survival (OS) benefit versus chemotherapy in all randomly assigned patients (5-year OS rates, 14% v 6%; hazard ratio [HR], 0.74 [95% CI, 0.62 to 0.88]); similar benefit was observed regardless of tumor histology. Of biomarker-evaluable patients treated with nivolumab plus ipilimumab (n = 242), high baseline monocytic myeloid-derived suppressor cell (M-MDSC) levels correlated with worse OS versus low M-MDSC levels (HR, 1.25 [95% CI, 1.09 to 1.43]). After adjusting for 24% of patients in the chemotherapy arm who received subsequent immunotherapy, nivolumab plus ipilimumab demonstrated continued OS benefit versus chemotherapy (HR, 0.64 [95% CI, 0.53 to 0.78]). No new safety signals were observed. These results demonstrate long-term, durable clinical benefit with nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy, which continued to be preserved even after treatment-switching adjustment in the chemotherapy arm. Exploratory analyses suggested greater benefit with nivolumab plus ipilimumab in the low M-MDSC subgroup. These results further support first-line nivolumab plus ipilimumab as standard of care for unresectable PM.
journal: Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology
citation: Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology. 2026;44(9):742-749

full_text_sections.INTRODUCTION: INTRODUCTION Nivolumab plus ipilimumab has demonstrated long-term, durable survival benefit as first-line treatment in patients with various solid tumor types. 1 - 3 The randomized phase III CheckMate 743 study demonstrated significant overall survival (OS) benefit with nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy in patients with unresectable pleural mesothelioma (PM; hazard ratio [HR], 0.74 [96.6% CI, 0.60 to 0.91] P = .0020). 4 Subsequently, nivolumab plus ipilimumab was approved as a first-line treatment option for patients with unresectable PM in several countries, including the United States and the European Union. 5 , 6 Four-year follow-up data from CheckMate 743 showed sustained OS benefit with nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy (HR, 0.73 [95% CI, 0.61 to 0.87]). 7 Here, we report 5-year clinical outcomes from CheckMate 743, the longest follow-up to date, to our knowledge, for first-line immunotherapy in PM. Additionally, we report exploratory biomarker analyses and treatment switching–adjusted analyses in patients who received subsequent immunotherapy after first-line chemotherapy.
full_text_sections.METHODS: METHODS The study design and patient eligibility criteria for CheckMate 743 are summarized in the Data Supplement (Fig S1, online only). 4 Patients were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive nivolumab 3 mg/kg once every 2 weeks plus ipilimumab 1 mg/kg once every 6 weeks, or cisplatin 75 mg/m 2 or carboplatin area under the concentration-time curve 5 mg/mL per minute plus pemetrexed 500 mg/m 2 once every 3 weeks for a maximum of six cycles. This trial was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki and the International Conference on Harmonisation Good Clinical Practice Guidelines. The study protocol and amendments were approved by an institutional review board or independent ethics committee at each site. All patients provided written informed consent. Assessments included 5-year OS, progression-free survival (PFS), objective response rate (ORR), and duration of response (DOR) in all randomly assigned patients and by tumor histology and safety. Additionally, we report OS and PFS by baseline monocytic myeloid-derived suppressor cell (M-MDSC) level and OS adjusted for treatment switching among patients in the chemotherapy arm. Additional methodology information is included in the Data Supplement.
full_text_sections.RESULTS: RESULTS Patients and Treatment Summary Overall, 303 and 302 patients were randomly assigned to the nivolumab plus ipilimumab and chemotherapy arms, respectively; 300 (99%) and 284 (94%) patients received the respective study treatments (Fig 1 ). 4 Baseline characteristics were similar across treatment arms (Table 1 ). Among randomly assigned patients, 6% of patients treated with nivolumab plus ipilimumab and 24% of patients treated with chemotherapy received subsequent immunotherapy (Data Supplement, Table S1). FIG 1. CONSORT diagram. TABLE 1. Baseline Characteristics in All Randomly Assigned Patients and by Tumor Histology Characteristic All Randomly Assigned Epithelioid Nonepithelioid Nivolumab Plus Ipilimumab (n = 303) Chemotherapy (n = 302) Nivolumab Plus Ipilimumab (n = 229) Chemotherapy (n = 226) Nivolumab Plus Ipilimumab (n = 74) Chemotherapy (n = 76) Age, years, median (IQR) 69 (65-75) 69 (62-75) 69 (64-75) 69 (62-74) 70 (67-74) 70 (64-76) Sex, No. (%) Male 234 (77) 233 (77) 172 (75) 171 (76) 62 (84) 62 (82) Female 69 (23) 29 (23) 57 (25) 55 (24) 12 (16) 14 (19) Region, No. (%) North America 32 (11) 27 (9) 22 (10) 17 (8) 10 (14) 10 (13) Europe 177 (58) 175 (58) 144 (63) 133 (59) 33 (45) 42 (55) Asia 26 (9) 39 (13) 11 (5) 20 (9) 15 (20) 19 (25) Rest of the world a 68 (22) 61 (20) 52 (23) 56 (25) 16 (22) 5 (7) ECOG PS, b No. (%) 0 114 (38) 128 (42) 92 (40) 106 (47) 22 (30) 22 (29) 1 189 (62) 173 (57) 137 (60) 120 (53) 52 (70) 53 (70) Smoking status, No. (%) Current/former 173 (57) 171 (57) 126 (55) 122 (54) 47 (64) 49 (64) Never 127 (42) 122 (40) 100 (44) 96 (42) 27 (36) 26 (34) Unknown 3 (1) 9 (3) 3 (1) 8 (4) 0 1 (1) Tumor histology, No. (%) Epithelioid 229 (76) 226 (75) 229 (100) 226 (100) 0 0 Nonepithelioid 74 (24) 76 (25) 0 0 74 (100) 76 (100) Sarcomatoid 35 (12) 36 (12) 0 0 35 (47) 36 (47) Mixed/other 39 (13) 39 (13) 0 0 39 (53) 40 (53) Stage, No. (%) I 12 (4) 20 (7) 8 (4) 15 (7) 4 (5) 5 (7) II 23 (8) 22 (7) 15 (7) 16 (7) 8 (11) 6 (8) III 103 (34) 106 (35) 85 (37) 84 (37) 18 (24) 22 (29) IV 160 (53) 149 (49) 116 (51) 107 (47) 44 (60) 42 (55) Not reported 5 (2) 5 (2) 5 (2) 4 (2) 0 1 (1) Abbreviation: ECOG PS, Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status. a Australia, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, South Africa, and Turkey. b On a score of 0 to 5, with higher scores indicating greater disability. One patient in the chemotherapy group had a baseline ECOG PS of 2 (protocol deviation). Efficacy At database lock (June 14, 2023; median follow-up for OS, 66.8 months), nivolumab plus ipilimumab demonstrated continued OS benefit versus chemotherapy (HR, 0.74 [95% CI, 0.62 to 0.88]); 5-year OS rates were 14% versus 6% (Fig 2 A). Consistent OS benefit with nivolumab plus ipilimumab was observed across most subgroups (Fig 2 B). Five-year OS rates with nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy were 14% versus 8% in patients with epithelioid PM (HR, 0.85 [95% CI, 0.69 to 1.03]; Fig 2 C) and 12% versus 1% in patients with nonepithelioid PM (HR, 0.48 [95% CI, 0.33 to 0.68]; Fig 2 D). FIG 2. OS in (A) all randomly assigned patients, (B) key patient subgroups, (C) patients with epithelioid tumor histology, and (D) patients with nonepithelioid tumor histology. The 95% CIs for 5-year OS rates for all randomly assigned patients, patients with epithelioid PM, and patients with nonepithelioid PM in the nivolumab plus ipilimumab and chemotherapy arms were as follows: a 10-18; b 4-10; c 10-19; d 5-12; e 6-22; and f 0-7. ECOG PS, Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status; HR, hazard ratio; OS, overall survival; PM, pleural mesothelioma. Continued PFS improvement was observed with nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy in all randomly assigned patients (HR, 0.93 [95% CI, 0.77 to 1.13]); the 5-year PFS rate was 8% for nivolumab plus ipilimumab, whereas all patients in the chemotherapy arm were censored or experienced a PFS event before 5 years (Data Supplement, Fig S2A). PFS analyses by tumor histology are reported in the Data Supplement (Figs S2B and S2C). ORRs were 39% with nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus 44% with chemotherapy in all randomly assigned patients (Data Supplement, Table S2). Of responders, DOR favored nivolumab plus ipilimumab over chemotherapy (Data Supplement, Fig S3A). At 5 years, 17% of responders treated with nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus 0% of responders treated with chemotherapy demonstrated ongoing response. Similar trends toward improved, sustained response with nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy were observed across tumor histology subgroups (Data Supplement, Figs S3B and S3C). Biomarker Analyses Of the randomly assigned patients, 242 (80%) patients treated with nivolumab plus ipilimumab and 218 (72%) patients treated with chemotherapy had evaluable blood M-MDSC measurements (Data Supplement, Table S3). In the M-MDSC–evaluable population, nivolumab plus ipilimumab improved OS versus chemotherapy (median [95% CI], 19.6 months [17.3 to 24.9] versus 14.9 months [12.3 to 16.5]; HR, 0.72 [95% CI, 0.59 to 0.88]; Fig 3 A); PFS favored nivolumab plus ipilimumab over chemotherapy (Fig 3 B). Among patients treated with nivolumab plus ipilimumab, higher M-MDSC levels were associated with worse OS and PFS outcomes in M-MDSC–evaluable patients (OS HR, 1.25 [95% CI, 1.09 to 1.43]; PFS HR, 1.23 [95% CI, 1.06 to 1.44]) and the epithelioid PM subgroup (OS HR, 1.27 [95% CI, 1.09 to 1.48]; PFS HR, 1.26 [95% CI, 1.06 to 1.50]; Fig 3 C). FIG 3. (A) OS and (B) PFS in the M-MDSC–evaluable population and (C) associations between blood M-MDSC with OS and PFS in the M-MDSC–evaluable population and tumor histology subgroups. The 95% CIs for 5-year OS and PFS rates in the nivolumab plus ipilimumab and chemotherapy arms were as follows: a 11-20; b 4-11; c 4-13; d NA-NA. All subgroups included in the forest plot were predetermined at baseline before random assignment. HRs for each 1-unit increase in log-transformed blood M-MDSC levels were reported. An HR >1 indicated increased risk of an event with higher M-MDSC levels, whereas an HR <1 indicated decreased risk. HR, hazard ratio; M-MDSC, monocytic myeloid-derived suppressor cell; NA, not applicable/available; OS, overall survival; PFS, progression-free survival. Treatment Switching–Adjusted Analyses Of patients randomly assigned to the chemotherapy arm, 72 (24%) patients received subsequent immunotherapy. Adjusting for subsequent immunotherapy use in the chemotherapy arm using the inverse probability of censoring weighting (IPCW) method, median OS (95% CI) with chemotherapy was 12.1 months (10.2 to 13.9), and the adjusted HR (95% CI) for nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy was 0.64 (0.53 to 0.78; Data Supplement, Fig S4A). Consistent results were observed in sensitivity analyses (Data Supplement, Fig S5). Treatment switching–adjusted analyses by tumor histology are reported in the Data Supplement (Supplemental Results; Figs S4B, S4C, S6, and S7). Safety Consistent with previous reports, 4 , 8 no new safety signals were identified, as summarized in the Data Supplement (Tables S4-S6).
full_text_sections.DISCUSSION: DISCUSSION With a median follow-up of 66.8 months, nivolumab plus ipilimumab demonstrated long-term, durable survival and response benefit versus chemotherapy in all randomly assigned patients and across most subgroups. In patients with evaluable blood M-MDSC measurements, elevated baseline M-MDSC levels trended toward reduced benefit. After adjusting for treatment switching in the chemotherapy arm, a greater magnitude in OS benefit with nivolumab plus ipilimumab was observed. Improved survival and response outcomes with nivolumab plus ipilimumab were consistent with or better than other first-line immunotherapy-based treatments for unresectable PM. 9 - 11 In a meta-analysis comparing six randomized controlled trials of first-line treatments for PM, nivolumab plus ipilimumab significantly improved OS versus chemotherapy, similar to benefit with bevacizumab plus chemotherapy or pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy. 12 The magnitude of clinical benefit with nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy was greater in patients with nonepithelioid PM versus epithelioid PM in CheckMate 743, consistent with results for pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy versus chemotherapy in KEYNOTE-483, and may have resulted from differences in immune-related gene expression across tumor histology subgroups, higher levels of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes in nonepithelioid PM, and/or worse OS with chemotherapy in patients with nonepithelioid PM. 13 , 14 M-MDSCs have been associated with immunotherapy resistance and worse prognoses in patients with PM. 15 - 20 In CheckMate 743, OS and PFS with nivolumab plus ipilimumab were worse in patients with high M-MDSC levels in all randomly assigned patients and patients with epithelioid PM; however, additional prospective studies are needed to confirm correlations between M-MDSC levels and clinical outcomes with first-line immunotherapy. Patients with relapsing PM after first-line chemotherapy may benefit from second-line treatment. 21 - 24 The IPCW-adjusted median OS analysis in the chemotherapy group decreased from 14.1 months to 12.1 months, amplifying the clinical benefit of nivolumab plus ipilimumab (HR adjusted from 0.75 to 0.64). In conclusion, this 5-year update from CheckMate 743 demonstrated continued long-term, durable clinical benefit of first-line nivolumab plus ipilimumab in patients with PM, regardless of tumor histology. Future prospective analyses are needed to evaluate the prognostic or predictive value of M-MDSCs in patients with PM treated with first-line immunotherapy and their role in the treatment selection process beyond current guidelines. 25
